title: Forwards vs Futures

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: A forward and a futures contract on the same underlying share the same theoretical price F = S * exp((r + u - y) * T) under DETERMINISTIC interest rates. Now allow rates to be stochastic.
rules: Forward: settles only at maturity (single cash flow).
Futures: marked-to-market DAILY -- profits/losses realized along the way.
Daily P&L can be reinvested (or financed) at the prevailing short rate.
setup.question: If the asset price is positively correlated with interest rates, which contract has higher price?

learningObjectives: Distinguish forward and futures cash-flow timing.
Predict the sign of the forward-futures spread under positive / negative rate correlation.

stages.label: Cash-flow comparison
stages.prompt: What's the FUNDAMENTAL difference in cash-flow structure between a forward and a futures?

options.text: Forward: one cash flow at maturity. Futures: daily mark-to-market means many small cash flows along the way.
Forwards have margin requirements; futures don't.

stages.explanation: Forwards: zero cash flow until maturity. Futures: daily settlement of P&L through margin accounts. Actual cash exchanges along the way matter when rates are stochastic.

stages.label: Correlation argument
stages.prompt: If F and r are POSITIVELY correlated (F up <-> r up): when does the futures-holder receive cash, and at what rate is it reinvested?

options.text: When F rises (gain): r is high, so the gain is reinvested at HIGH rates. When F falls (loss): r is low, so the loss is financed at LOW rates. Asymmetry favors the futures.
Cash flows happen randomly, no pattern with rates.

stages.explanation: The mark-to-market mechanism gives the futures holder a TIMING advantage when corr(F, r) > 0: gains compound at high rates, losses are funded cheaply. The forward contract has no such timing benefit since it settles only once.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: If corr(F, r) > 0: which contract has higher price (futures price minus forward price > 0 or < 0)?

options.text: Futures > Forward. Positive correlation makes futures more valuable.

stages.explanation: Futures price > Forward price under positive corr(F, r). With negative correlation, the inequality flips: futures < forward. With deterministic rates (correlation undefined), futures = forward.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Apply this to BOND futures specifically. (Bond price corr with rates is NEGATIVE, so bond futures < bond forwards in theory.)
Why is the spread negligible for short-dated futures? (Less time for the timing advantage to compound; small.)
Other differences: counterparty risk (futures cleared, forwards bilateral), liquidity (futures often deeper), customization (forwards flexible).
